prompt: Write me a TDD for the logsql CLI tool, PRD at /Users/nhoze/.claude/skills/tech-lead/evals/prd-logsql-cli.md. I want the design to take a real stance on the things engineers will fight about (Go vs Python, parser strictness, schema-on-read vs config) instead of punting all of them. Output as a .md file in the current dir.

assertions.text: A single TDD-*.md file exists in the outputs/ directory
Document is at least 1500 words
Contains a 'Problem' / 'Context' / 'Background' section
Contains a 'Scope' section with both in-scope and out-of-scope items
Contains a 'High-level approach' / 'Overview' section
Contains a 'Detailed design' section with substantive content
Contains 'Alternatives considered' with at least 2 alternatives and rejection rationale for each
Contains an NFRs section with concrete targets (numbers/SLOs)
Contains a 'Risks' / 'Failure modes' section
Contains a 'Rollout' section appropriate for a CLI (release / distribution / versioning)
Contains 'Open questions' with at least one entry
No fabricated/invented URLs to internal systems
TDD is structured as a CLI utility design
Sketches the command surface with flags / args / --help shape
Defines exit codes (incl. any tool-specific codes as a deliberate design choice)
Documents STDIN / pipe composition behavior (reads stdin when no files; works in pipelines)
Explicitly states streaming vs whole-load posture, with memory bound for aggregations (GROUP BY / ORDER BY)
Specifies output formats and how the user selects between them (--json/--csv/--tsv/etc.)
Takes an explicit position on Go vs Python with stated tradeoffs (does not punt to 'either is fine')
Takes an explicit position on SQL parser strictness with reasoning
'Alternatives considered' includes existing tools like jq, duckdb, awk, or similar — with honest rejection rationale
Explicitly omits NFRs that do not apply to a CLI (availability SLO, multi-region, disaster recovery) — states the omission rather than fabricating values
Addresses the 5s/1GB-compressed performance target with a real plan
